content.text:
@/Users/macbookpro/Projects/eaglepoint/w1t21/docs/questions.md 
A LedgerMint Digital Collectibles Exchange platform capable of running fully offline on a company LAN enables Buyers to browse limited-edition digital collectibles, place orders, and track fulfillment using a React web interface with an English UI optimized for desktop and tablet. Users authenticate with a local username and password, see immediate inline validation feedback, and land on a personalized home screen that highlights owned items, open orders, and unread notifications. Sellers can list collectibles with rich metadata previews and transaction history, then respond to order-level messages in an in-app chat thread that supports attachments limited to 10 MB and blocks sensitive content such as phone numbers and SSNs using on-device pattern detection before the message is sent. A Notification Center consolidates system updates like “Order Confirmed,” “Refund Approved,” “Arbitration Opened,” and “Review Posted,” and allows users to subscribe to templates (for example, “status changes only” vs “all events”) with clear read/unread state and a manual “retry delivery” action when a notice is flagged as failed. Administrators and Compliance Analysts use an internal analytics area to review funnels from view-to-order, 7/30-day retention, and content performance, and they can run A/B tests on UI variants (such as two item detail layouts) with deterministic traffic splitting, a visible start/end date like 03/25/2026 5:00 PM, and a one-click rollback to the prior configuration if conversion drops beyond a defined threshold.

The backend uses Go with Echo to expose REST-style APIs consumed by the React client, with PostgreSQL as the sole system of record for users, collectibles, orders, message threads, notifications, analytics events, and audit trails. API authentication supports JWT or server sessions, all state-changing requests enforce CSRF protection, and every request is subject to parameter validation and injection prevention. Security controls include rate limiting per user and per IP (for example, 10 login attempts per 15 minutes with a 30-minute lockout after 5 failures), IP allow/deny lists managed by Administrators, and anomalous behavior detection that flags accounts with patterns such as more than 6 cancellations in 24 hours or repeated “checkout attempt” failures, generating an internal alert visible in the admin console without relying on email or SMS. Sensitive data such as phone numbers or government IDs, if collected for compliance, is masked in the UI and encrypted at rest in PostgreSQL using application-managed keys stored in local encrypted config files; secrets never appear in logs. The domain model is standardized yet extensible, supporting collectible fields like contract address, chain identifier, TokenID, metadata URI, and immutable transaction history with integrity constraints and validation rules to prevent duplicates. Maintainability requirements include a unified error-code strategy, idempotency keys on order creation to prevent duplicates, PostgreSQL advisory locks to prevent overselling, hot-data caching in process with defined TTLs (for example, 60 seconds for catalog tiles), asynchronous background jobs for notification retries and scheduled analytics rollups, and local-only monitoring via structured logs, metrics, and trace IDs persisted to disk for operator review.
Let me explore the current state of the repository first.
